summary:
The Charge of the Light Brigade on 25 October 1854 was a disastrous cavalry charge during the Battle of Balaclava in the [[event:2547|Crimean War]], in which the British Light Cavalry Brigade was ordered to charge directly into Russian artillery — the result of a miscommunication between commanders. Of the approximately 670 horsemen who charged, over 110 were killed and 160 wounded; many horses were also killed. The charge lasted barely twenty minutes but became one of the most famous episodes in British military history.

The confusion arose from an ambiguous order delivered by Captain Louis Nolan from Lord Raglan, the British commander, to Lord Lucan, who commanded the cavalry division, ordering the cavalry to prevent the Russians from carrying off captured guns. Raglan could see the larger battlefield from his position on the heights; Lucan and Lord Cardigan, commanding the Light Brigade, could not and misidentified which guns were meant. The result was a charge directly down a valley enfiladed by Russian artillery on three sides.

The charge was witnessed by French allied cavalry officers, one of whom reportedly said 'C'est magnifique, mais ce n'est pas la guerre' (It is magnificent, but it is not war). Alfred, Lord Tennyson's poem The Charge of the Light Brigade, published within weeks of the event, immortalized the suicidal courage of the troopers with its famous lines and established the charge as a symbol of both heroism and military incompetence. The event, along with the broader failings of the Crimean War's conduct, contributed to significant reforms in the British Army's command and supply systems.
